questions.prompt: Why should you use IAM roles for EC2 instances rather than putting AWS access keys on the instance?

choices.text: Security and rotation. An IAM role provides temporary, automatically-rotated credentials to the instance via the metadata service — AWS SDKs use them transparently. Access keys, by contrast, are long-term credentials that don't expire automatically; if leaked (via SSH access, a compromised process, a misconfigured logging system, or a developer accidentally putting them in code), the attacker has indefinite access until manually rotated. Roles also enable least-privilege at the workload level — each ASG or service gets its own role with exactly the permissions it needs. Keys often end up over-permissioned because they're a hassle to create per-use-case. The metadata service has its own security model (IMDSv2 with token-based auth is the modern default to prevent SSRF attacks). For any EC2 instance calling AWS APIs, an attached IAM role is the right pattern; long-term access keys on EC2 instances are an anti-pattern that produces avoidable breaches.

questions.explanation: IAM roles + temporary credentials are the modern pattern. Long-term access keys on EC2 are 2015-era thinking that produces preventable security incidents.

questions.prompt: A team runs production on 10 individually-managed EC2 instances. One fails; users notice; engineers manually launch a replacement. What's the structural fix?

choices.text: Use an Auto Scaling Group (ASG) with a Launch Template, behind a load balancer, spanning multiple AZs. The ASG maintains the desired instance count: when one fails health checks, the ASG terminates it and launches a replacement automatically. Combined with multi-AZ distribution, a single AZ failure doesn't take down the application — the ASG keeps healthy instances in other AZs serving traffic and launches replacements as that AZ recovers. The Launch Template captures all instance configuration (AMI, type, security groups, IAM role, user data) so replacements are identical. The load balancer routes traffic only to healthy instances. This combination is the production baseline for EC2 — individually-managed instances are appropriate for one-off tools or short experiments, never for production workloads. Adding scaling policies (CPU-based, request-based) makes the system handle load changes too.

questions.explanation: ASGs + Launch Templates + Load Balancers + Multi-AZ is the production EC2 pattern. Manual instance management produces preventable outages and operational toil.
